skill.name: terraform-module-library
skill.description: Build reusable Terraform modules for AWS, Azure, and GCP infrastructure following infrastructure-as-code best practices.
skill.summary: Create production-ready Terraform modules for multi-cloud infrastructure with standardized patterns and testing.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 58 static analysis findings are false positives. The 'backtick execution' patterns are markdown code fences, 'path traversal' sequences are Terraform module references, 'hardcoded IPs' are private CIDR examples, and 'weak crypto' matches are HCL validation regex patterns. The skill contains only documentation and Terraform configuration examples with no executable code or security risks.

content.user_title: Build Reusable Terraform Modules for Multi-Cloud Infrastructure
content.value_statement: Infrastructure teams struggle with inconsistent, one-off Terraform configurations that are hard to maintain. This skill provides production-ready module patterns for AWS, Azure, and GCP that standardize cloud provisioning across your organization.
actual_capabilities: Generate Terraform module structures for AWS VPC, EKS, RDS, and S3 resources
Create Azure module patterns for VNet, AKS, and storage resources
Build GCP modules for VPC, GKE, and Cloud SQL configurations
Implement input validation with HCL validation blocks
Provide Terratest test templates for module validation
Document modules with README templates and usage examples
limitations: Does not execute Terraform commands or manage actual cloud resources
Does not validate cloud provider credentials or connectivity
Module patterns are reference implementations requiring customization for specific environments
Does not replace infrastructure security review or compliance auditing

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer Building Standard Modules
use_cases.description: Create a library of reusable VPC and EKS modules with consistent tagging, validation, and testing patterns for organization-wide use.

use_cases.title: Cloud Architect Establishing IaC Standards
use_cases.description: Define multi-cloud module patterns that enforce security best practices, naming conventions, and resource configurations across AWS, Azure, and GCP.

use_cases.title: Platform Team Automating Provisioning
use_cases.description: Build self-service module catalog enabling development teams to provision standardized infrastructure without deep Terraform expertise.

prompt_templates.title: Basic VPC Module
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Terraform VPC module for AWS with public and private subnets. Include variables for CIDR blocks, availability zones, and tags. Add output values for VPC ID and subnet IDs.

prompt_templates.title: RDS Module with Validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Terraform RDS module for PostgreSQL with input validation for engine version, instance class, and storage size. Include conditional read replica configuration and security group rules.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Cloud Storage Module
prompt_templates.prompt: Design equivalent storage modules for AWS S3, Azure Blob Storage, and GCP Cloud Storage. Each module should support versioning, encryption, lifecycle policies, and access logging with consistent variable names.

prompt_templates.title: Complete EKS Module with Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production-ready EKS module with managed node groups, IRSA configuration, and cluster logging. Include Terratest test files that validate cluster creation and outputs.

output_examples.input: Generate an AWS VPC module with two private subnets across availability zones
output_examples.output: Creates main.tf with VPC and subnet resources, variables.tf with CIDR and AZ parameters, outputs.tf exposing resource IDs, and validation rules for input formats.

output_examples.input: Add validation to ensure RDS instance class is from allowed list
output_examples.output: Adds HCL validation block checking instance_class against permitted values like db.t3.micro through db.r5.24xlarge with descriptive error message.

best_practices: Use semantic versioning for modules and pin provider versions in versions.tf
Document all variables with descriptions, types, and default values
Include working examples in examples/ directory and Terratest tests in tests/ directory
anti_patterns: Hardcoding values instead of using variables for environment-specific configuration
Omitting output values that module consumers need for composition
Writing modules without validation blocks for critical input parameters

faq.question: What Terraform version do these modules require?
faq.answer: Modules target Terraform 1.0+ with AWS provider version 5.x. Provider version constraints are specified in each module's versions.tf file.

faq.question: Can I use these modules with multiple cloud providers simultaneously?
faq.answer: Yes. Each module is provider-specific and can be combined in a single Terraform configuration by configuring multiple providers and calling modules from different cloud directories.

faq.question: How do I test modules before deploying to production?
faq.answer: Use the provided Terratest templates in the tests/ directory. Run tests against a sandbox AWS account and destroy resources after validation using terraform.Destroy in test teardown.

faq.question: Are these modules compatible with Terraform Cloud or Enterprise?
faq.answer: Yes. Modules follow standard Terraform patterns compatible with all Terraform execution environments including CLI, Cloud, and Enterprise with appropriate workspace configuration.

faq.question: How do I handle secrets in these modules?
faq.answer: Never hardcode secrets in module files. Use variables marked sensitive=true and pass values via environment variables, Terraform Cloud variables, or external secret management systems.

faq.question: Can I contribute improvements to these module patterns?
faq.answer: Yes. Follow the standard module structure, add tests for new features, update README documentation, and ensure examples demonstrate complete working configurations.
